metadata.title: What the Floor Hides
metadata.setting: A quiet English town in the present day, where an abandoned terrace house on Crane Street conceals a fifty-year-old secret.

characters.name: Jamie Cole
characters.description: A 20-year-old with dark hair and restless eyes who is quick to notice things other people overlook.
characters.flaw: Acts on impulse without thinking through the risk to himself or others, which consistently puts him in danger before he is ready.
characters.current_state: urgent and ready to act, aware that Voss has a head start back to her office and the locked evidence
key_items: old video camera (scratched casing, found wrapped in dark cloth behind the cupboard rag)

characters.name: Margaret Voss
characters.description: A 45-year-old estate agent with neat clothes and a businesslike manner who hired Jamie to clear the house quickly.
characters.flaw: Prioritises protecting her family's reputation above honesty, which makes her reckless when she feels cornered.
characters.current_state: composed but alarmed, having left Mrs. Holt's house after failing to prevent her from committing to a police statement; heading back to her office and the locked evidence
key_items: handwritten note dated 14 February 1974 (naming R. Voss in connection with planned arson; locked in desk drawer)
three black-and-white photographs (showing fire and smoke damage to the Crane Street house; locked in desk drawer)
opened metal box (locked in desk drawer at Voss Estate Agency)
small key to desk drawer (in her jacket pocket)

characters.name: Mrs. Holt
characters.description: A sharp-eyed woman in her mid-seventies who has lived next door to the abandoned house for decades and notices everything.
characters.flaw: Stays silent when speaking out would help, because she fears the trouble that telling the truth might bring.
characters.current_state: resolved and clear-headed, having finally committed to giving a police statement after fifty years of silence

locations.name: The Abandoned House, Crane Street
locations.description: A narrow two-storey terrace house, empty for decades, its walls streaked with damp and its hallway thick with the smell of old plaster.

locations.name: The Front Room
locations.description: The largest room in the house, with a wide bay window facing the street, wide bare floorboards, and a single old cupboard set into the wall beside the fireplace.

locations.name: Voss Estate Agency
locations.description: A glass-fronted office on the high street, always tidy, with a framed photo of the town on the wall and a strong smell of fresh coffee.

locations.name: Mrs. Holt's House
locations.description: The terrace house immediately next door, its front window offering a direct view of the Crane Street property's entrance and front room.

episode_history.summary: Jamie begins clearing the abandoned house on Crane Street and finds an old video camera tucked behind a rag in the front-room cupboard, wrapped in dark cloth. The camera footage shows an unidentified middle-aged man hiding a rectangular object beneath a floorboard near the bay window. When Jamie investigates the same floorboard, he finds the dust wiped away and fresh scratch marks along its edge, showing it has been disturbed recently — someone else has already located the hiding spot but left without taking what is there. Jamie says nothing to Margaret Voss when she calls, and as he stands by the window he notices Mrs. Holt next door watching him with silent, unbroken attention.

Jamie knocks on Mrs. Holt's door and shows her the camera footage; she identifies the man as Frank Warner and reveals that the Warner family — Frank, his wife, and their young daughter — vanished overnight from the house in 1974, with the property sitting empty ever since, before closing the door and refusing to say more. Back in the front room, Jamie lifts the third floorboard and retrieves a small metal box wrapped in grey cloth, its lock rusted solid; he hides it in his bag and replaces the floorboard so the room appears undisturbed. Margaret Voss arrives unexpectedly to check on progress and, while appearing casual, moves her eyes directly to the exact floorboard spot and holds them there one beat too long — leaving Jamie unable to tell whether she already knows the box is gone.

Voss, still in the front room, asked Jamie directly whether he had found anything unusual, then warned him to call her immediately if he did before departing. Once her car had left Crane Street, Jamie forced open the metal box with a screwdriver and found three black-and-white photographs of the house showing severe fire and smoke damage, and a sealed envelope containing a handwritten note dated 14 February 1974. The note names R. Voss at the top and describes a deliberate plan to clear the Crane Street property by arson while the Warners were still living there, constituting written evidence of the crime. Jamie sits with the opened evidence, aware that the name on the note matches the woman who just walked out and that she very likely suspects he has it.

Acting on impulse, Jamie took the note and photographs to Voss Estate Agency to confront Voss directly. Voss did not deny the evidence; she revealed that R. Voss is her father, Robert Voss, dead for twenty years, and confirmed she had entered the Crane Street house before Jamie's first day, found the loose floorboard, and left without the box only because she ran out of time. She then collected the note, the photographs, and the box from her desk, locked them in her desk drawer, pocketed the key, and warned Jamie she expected not to hear from him again. Jamie left without the money she offered and without the physical evidence, but with the camera still in his bag — an item Voss never asked about and does not know exists. He walked back toward Crane Street and stopped at Mrs. Holt's door.

Jamie showed Mrs. Holt the camera and told her Voss had taken all the physical evidence; Mrs. Holt then revealed that in February 1974 she had been awake and had witnessed the Warner family quietly fleeing with bags, the fire starting an hour later, and Robert Voss's car parked at the kerb — making her the only eyewitness to all three events. She admitted that Robert Voss had come to her door the following morning and warned her to stay silent, and that she had been protecting herself out of fear ever since. Voss arrived uninvited at Mrs. Holt's house, saw the camera on the low table, and attempted to intimidate them both before leaving; Mrs. Holt declared she would give a statement to the police, and Jamie told her they needed to go immediately.

unresolved_threads.description: Voss has taken the note, photographs, and box and locked them away; Jamie has only the camera, which Voss does not know about, and Mrs. Holt's eyewitness testimony. Whether Jamie and Mrs. Holt can reach the police before Voss moves or destroys the physical evidence — and what Voss will do when cornered — remains unresolved.

series_plan.central_question: What secret did the Warner family hide in that house fifty years ago, and who is willing to bury it again today?

arc_beats.beat: Jamie finds the old camera, watches the footage of the hiding, and discovers the floorboard has already been recently disturbed — someone else knows.
Jamie finds the metal box still hidden beneath the floor, and Mrs. Holt tells him who the family on the tape were — the Warners, who vanished from the street overnight in 1974.
Jamie opens the box and finds photographs and a handwritten note that point to a deliberate arson in 1974 — and the name on the note is Voss.
Margaret confronts Jamie, takes the box from him, and warns him to stop; Jamie is left with nothing physical — only the camera footage.
Jamie uses the camera footage and Mrs. Holt's testimony to force the truth into the open, facing direct intimidation from Margaret.
The truth about the 1974 arson and the Warner family's disappearance is exposed; consequences fall on those responsible, and Jamie's choice to act despite real risk is shown to matter.

from_targets.context: "She had been a witness to all of it — the family fleeing, the fire, the car — and she had kept it to herself for fifty years."
"If you gave a statement to the police, the camera footage would support it." / "I'll be giving a statement to the police about what I saw that night on Crane Street."
"They were fleeing, but quietly. It was deliberate. They knew exactly where they were going."
"It was deliberate. They knew exactly where they were going." / "It shows the box was hidden deliberately — that there was something to hide."
"He kept his voice steady." / "Her voice was steady."
"Voss had admitted her father had planned the arson deliberately"
"I've been protecting myself ever since. I told myself I was protecting my family, but really it was myself."

last_scene_position: The episode ends in Mrs. Holt's front room (loc_004), where Jamie has just turned from the window after watching Voss's car pull away and turn the corner at the end of the street. The old camera sits on the low table between the two armchairs. Jamie has just said 'We need to go now' to Mrs. Holt, who has committed to giving a statement to the police; Voss is in transit back to Voss Estate Agency (loc_003) and her locked desk drawer, with a head start on them. Episode 6 begins after this moment.
